---
title: "Three Poems from *The Bou Regreg*"
subtitle: "New poems by Yasmin Ighil."
description: "Three meditations on the river that flows through Rabat, where history and current meet."
author: "Mehdi Khribch"
date: "2026-03-21"
categories: [Poetry]
image: https://picsum.photos/seed/rrb-bou-regreg/1200/800
image-alt: "Placeholder"
---

The Bou Regreg is not merely a river—it is a keeper of contradictions. It flows through Rabat with the patience of a witness, having carried dynasties, trade, sorrow, and renewal. Yasmin Ighil, whose family traces roots to the Atlas foothills, approaches the river not as metaphor but as presence: a constant, tidal voice beneath the city's surface. These three poems move from the river's geological patience to its domestic intimacy, asking what it means to live beside something that is always leaving.

### The Mouth

<div class="rrb-poem">

Where the river meets the grey Atlantic,
the water forgets its name. The salt rises
to meet it halfway—two forgettings
becoming one. I have stood there
in the glistening hour before dusk,
watching the fresh turn brackish,
turn indifferent. The herons do not hesitate.
They wade as if they own the covenant
between two elements learning to surrender.

</div>

### Laundry Day

<div class="rrb-poem">

My grandmother knelt at the concrete lip
where the river runs, her hands
working cloth against the grooved stone
as if she were speaking to someone downstream.
The water was cold, even in August.
She never once looked up at the medina walls
or the palace, though they looked down at her.
The soap—olive-colored, the size of her fist—
turned to foam. The cloth darkened. Then slowly,
like a secret, turned white again.

</div>

### Current

<div class="rrb-poem">

At night the river knows something the city does not.
It refuses to be still. Even in drought,
even when the engineers speak of scarcity,
it mutters its complaint in the darkness—
a kind of prayer, a kind of refusal.
My son dropped his blue marble in once,
years ago. I told him it was traveling
to the sea, to Spain, to places without names.
He believed me. Sometimes I drive to the mouth
and stand in the spray, looking,
as if that marble might surface,
blue and worn, demanding to be known again.

</div>

---

*Yasmin Ighil was born in Fez and studied classical Arabic literature at the University of Rabat before moving to Casablanca to work in cultural preservation. Her first collection, "Stone Memory," won the 2025 Tanger Prize. She lives near the Bou Regreg.*
